articles.title: Cretaceous Legged Snake Najash rionegrina: Implications for Snake Evolution and the Phylogenetic History of the Jugal Bone
articles.summary: A remarkably preserved fossil of Najash rionegrina from Argentina, dating to the Late Cretaceous (~100 Ma), provides critical insights into the early evolution of snakes. The specimen exhibits hind limbs and a well-developed jugal bone (cheekbone), a feature that has been lost in modern snakes. Using micro-computed tomography (micro-CT), researchers reconstructed the skull in exceptional detail, revealing neurovascular pathways and previously obscured bones. This study, published in Science Advances in 2019, challenges the prevailing hypothesis that snakes evolved from small, burrowing ancestors. Instead, the evidence supports a large-bodied, wide-mouthed ancestral morphology. The research also clarifies a long-standing anatomical debate: for 160 years, the jugal bone in snakes and related lizards was misidentified. The Najash fossils provide empirical evidence to correct this error, demonstrating a gradual loss of bones leading to the specialized skull of modern snakes. The study highlights that early snakes retained hindlimbs for an extended period before the evolution of limbless forms. Subsequent research, including the 2020 description of Boipeba tayasuensis (a large blind snake from Brazil) and a 2023 study reconstructing brain morphology of fossil and extant squamates, supports the complexity of snake origins. A 2025 study on a Middle Jurassic squamate from Scotland further indicates that early squamate evolution involved considerable anatomical experimentation and convergent evolution. These findings collectively underscore that snake evolution was not a linear progression but a complex process with multiple adaptive pathways.

articles.title: 132-Million-Year-Old Dinosaur Tracks from the Early Cretaceous of South Africa: Filling a Gap in the Mesozoic Record
articles.summary: A team of ichnologists has reported the discovery of dinosaur tracks in the early Cretaceous Brenton Formation along the Western Cape coast of South Africa, near Knysna. The tracks, estimated to be approximately 132 million years old, represent the youngest known dinosaur tracks in southern Africa, predating previous records by 50 million years. The site, a small outcrop measuring 40 by 5 meters, yielded dozens of theropod footprints preserved both as surface impressions and cross-sections within the cliff face. This discovery is particularly significant because the post-Jurassic fossil record in southern Africa is notoriously sparse, largely due to extensive volcanic activity from the Drakensberg Group eruptions around 182 million years ago, which buried many earlier fossil-bearing strata. The tracks provide direct evidence that dinosaurs persisted in the region well into the Cretaceous Period, challenging previous assumptions about their decline after the volcanic events. The researchers, initially searching for a theropod tooth previously found at the site, instead encountered the tracks, highlighting the serendipitous nature of paleontological fieldwork. This is only the second documented occurrence of Cretaceous dinosaur tracks in South Africa and underscores the importance of coastal exposures for understanding Mesozoic biodiversity. The findings contribute to a broader understanding of dinosaur distribution and survival in the aftermath of large-scale volcanic episodes.

articles.title: First Multilateral Talks on Fossil Fuel Phase-Out Emerge Amidst UN Climate Gridlock
articles.summary: In a significant development, approximately 60 nations, including several major oil producers, are convening in Santa Marta, Colombia, to deliberate on a comprehensive transition away from fossil fuels. This initiative marks the first multilateral effort to address the complete phase-out of coal, oil, and gas, an objective that has eluded the annual United Nations COP climate summits due to the requirement of unanimous consent, effectively granting veto power to major fossil fuel-producing states. Notable absentees include the United States, China, and India—collectively responsible for a substantial share of global emissions. The urgency of the meeting is underscored by dire scientific warnings: Prof. Johan Rockström of the Potsdam Institute for Climate Impact Research asserts that the 1.5°C warming threshold will likely be breached within three to five years, precipitating more frequent and severe extreme weather events and potentially triggering irreversible tipping points in Earth systems. Participants view the gathering as a complementary mechanism to the COP process, aiming to demonstrate that a critical mass of countries is committed to renewable energy and to catalyze action among hesitant nations. The UK Climate Envoy, Rachel Kyte, emphasized the importance of sharing transitional experiences to bolster energy security. The meeting reflects growing frustration with the pace of UN negotiations and the influence of fossil fuel interests, as well as the geopolitical shifts in energy policy, particularly the US retreat from climate leadership under President Trump. The outcome of these talks could shape the trajectory of global climate action and the feasibility of meeting the Paris Agreement goals.

articles.title: Non-Invasive Detection of Concealed Voids in Menkaure Pyramid Supports Hypothesis of Secondary Entrance
articles.summary: A collaborative research team from Cairo University and the Technical University of Munich (TUM), operating under the auspices of the ScanPyramids project, has identified two previously unknown air-filled cavities within the eastern facade of the Menkaure pyramid at Giza. The eastern face has been a subject of intrigue due to a section of granite blocks approximately four meters high and six meters wide that exhibits an anomalous smooth and polished finish, reminiscent of the known entrance on the northern side. In 2019, researcher Stijn van den Hoven postulated the existence of a second entrance at this location. To test this hypothesis, the team employed a multi-modal non-invasive survey combining ground-penetrating radar, ultrasound, and electrical resistivity tomography (ERT). The integration of these techniques, facilitated by Image Fusion, enabled precise localization of two voids at depths of 1.4 meters and 1.13 meters behind the outer wall. The larger void measures roughly 1 meter in height and 1.5 meters in width, while the smaller is approximately 0.9 meters by 0.7 meters. These findings provide the first concrete evidence supporting the presence of structural anomalies behind the polished section, thereby strengthening the entrance hypothesis. Professor Christian Grosse of TUM emphasized that the methodology allows accurate characterization of internal features without compromising the integrity of the monument. This discovery follows the 2023 detection of a hidden corridor in the Great Pyramid of Khufu, underscoring the efficacy of the ScanPyramids approach. The results have significant implications for Egyptology, potentially revealing new insights into pyramid construction and the ritual significance of multiple entrances.

articles.title: Enhancing Carbon Nanotube Conductivity: A Step Toward Copper-Competitive Wiring
articles.summary: Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) have long been heralded as a revolutionary material due to their exceptional mechanical strength and electrical properties. However, practical applications have been hindered by difficulties in achieving high electrical conductivity comparable to conventional metals like copper. A recent study published in Science presents a significant advancement: researchers doped bundles of double-walled carbon nanotubes with tetrachloroaluminate (AlCl4–) to enhance their current-carrying capacity. The doping process involved exposing commercial CNT fibers to a vapor of aluminum trichloride and chlorine, which infiltrated the interstitial spaces and formed AlCl4– in situ. This electron-donating dopant increased the mean conductivity by a factor of 10, with the highest-performing fibers achieving conductivity approximately 70% that of aluminum and 45% that of copper. Crucially, due to the low density of CNTs, the doped fibers outperformed copper in conductivity per unit mass. However, the enhanced conductivity was not stable; the material degraded over time, likely due to the dopant's reactivity. The study combined experimental characterization (spectroscopy, imaging) with density functional theory modeling to confirm the doping mechanism. While not immediately practical, this work identifies a promising dopant strategy and underscores the need for stable doping approaches to realize CNT-based wiring for applications in aerospace, electronics, and lightweight power transmission.

articles.title: The Persistent Anomaly of the Gravitational Constant: A Replication Study
articles.summary: The gravitational constant, denoted G and colloquially known as 'Big G,' is a fundamental parameter in physics that quantifies the strength of gravitational interaction. Despite its foundational role, G is the least precisely measured fundamental constant, with experimental values differing by approximately 0.01%. This imprecision stems from the intrinsic weakness of gravity relative to other forces, compounded by systematic errors and background noise from Earth's gravitational field (little g). In a recent replication study published in Metrologia, researchers at the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) revisited a 2007 torsion-balance experiment conducted by the International Bureau of Weights and Measures (BIPM), which had yielded a value significantly divergent from the consensus. The NIST team constructed a sophisticated torsion balance featuring eight metal cylinders: four outer masses on a rotating carousel and four inner masses suspended by a thin copper-beryllium ribbon. They measured gravitational torque by tracking the rotation of the inner masses. Additionally, they applied a voltage to electrodes beside the inner masses to generate an electrostatic torque opposing the gravitational torque, providing an independent estimate of G. To assess material-dependent systematic errors, they performed the experiment using both copper and sapphire masses, obtaining nearly identical values. Despite these rigorous controls, their result remained inconsistent with other high-precision measurements, indicating unresolved systematic errors. This study underscores the challenges in metrology and the necessity for diverse experimental approaches to converge on a definitive value for G.
